title: Angoulême International Comics Festival

infobox.value: Francis Groux, Jean Mardikian, Claude Moliterni
around 200,000 on average[[1]](#cite_note-1) and more than 220,000 in 2012[[2]](#cite_note-attendancefigaro-2)

(**AICF**; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Festival international de la bande dessinée d'Angoulême*, *FIBD*), held in [Angoulême](/wiki/Angoul%C3%AAme "Angoulême"), France, is the second largest [comics](/wiki/Comics "Comics") festival in Europe after the [Lucca Comics & Games](/wiki/Lucca_Comics_%26_Games "Lucca Comics & Games") of Italy, and the third biggest in the world after Lucca and the [Comiket](/wiki/Comiket "Comiket") of Japan.[[4]](#cite_note-official1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-mausangoul-5)[[6]](#cite_note-blcoshtabw-6) It has occurred every year since 1974, on the last weekend of January; the 2026 edition was cancelled amidst an artists' boycott to the festival organizer 9emeArt+, accused of mismanagement.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

awards, after the pet [auk](/wiki/Auk "Auk") from *[Zig et Puce](/wiki/Zig_et_Puce "Zig et Puce")* by [Alain Saint-Ogan](/wiki/Alain_Saint-Ogan "Alain Saint-Ogan"). In 1989, the name changed to the **Alph-art** awards, honoring the final, [unfinished *Tintin* album](/wiki/Tintin_and_Alph-Art "Tintin and Alph-Art") by [Hergé](/wiki/Herg%C3%A9 "Hergé"). In 2003, the Alph-art name was dropped, and they are now simply called "The Official Awards of the International Comics Festival" (*le Palmarès Officiel du Festival international de la bande dessinée*). In 2007, [Lewis Trondheim](/wiki/Lewis_Trondheim "Lewis Trondheim") (2006 Grand Prix winner) created a mascot for the festival, *Le Fauve* (The Wildcat), and since 2008 the prize winners have received wildcat statuettes, with the Best Album statuette [coated in gold](/wiki/Gilding "Gilding"). Since this year, the award is called the *fauve* and the best album, the *fauve d'or*. The prizes were reorganized too, to create a pool of 40-60 albums, called "official selections", from which are awarded the "Best Album" prize, five "Angoulême Essentials", one "Revelation Essential" (given to rookie creators), and one Essential chosen by the public. The Heritage Essential (for reprinted material) and Youth Essential are selected from separate nominee pools.

Bell, Anne-Laure. ["Angouleme International Comics Festival"](https://web.archive.org/web/20110715212903/http://www.ambafrance-uk.org/Culture-Angouleme-International). French embassy in the United Kingdom. Archived from [the original](http://www.ambafrance-uk.org/Culture-Angouleme-International) on 15 July 2011. Retrieved 30 January 2012.

Delcroix, Olivier (2012-01-29). ["Angoulême : la BD en pleine forme"](http://www.lefigaro.fr/bd/2012/01/29/03014-20120129ARTFIG00159-angouleme-la-bd-en-pleine-forme.php). *[Le Figaro](/wiki/Le_Figaro "Le Figaro")* (in French). Retrieved 30 January 2012.

Alverson, Brigid. ["10 creators withdraw names from Angouleme Grand Prix list,"](https://web.archive.org/web/20160107024018/http://robot6.comicbookresources.com/2016/01/nine-creators-withdraw-names-from-angouleme-grand-prix-list/) Robot 6: *Comic Book Resources* (January 6, 2016).
Lancel, Xavier (25 January 2012). ["Seven Hours Till Angoulême"](https://www.bleedingcool.com/2012/01/25/seven-hours-till-angoulme/). Scarce/Bleeding Cool. Retrieved 30 January 2012.

Miansoni, Sarah (2025-12-03). ["Angoulême Comics Festival cancels 2026 edition after authors call for boycott"](https://www.euronews.com/culture/2025/12/03/angouleme-comics-festival-cancels-2026-edition-after-authors-call-for-boycott). *www.euronews.com*. Retrieved 2025-12-20.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

French comics convention

| Angoulême International Comics Festival  Festival international de la bande dessinée d'Angoulême | |
| --- | --- |
| Pictures from 44th Angoulême festival in 2017 | |
| Status | active |
| Genre | [Comics](/wiki/Comics "Comics") |
| Date | late January |
| Frequency | annual |
| Location | [Angoulême](/wiki/Angoul%C3%AAme "Angoulême") |
| Country | [France](/wiki/France "France") |
| Years active | 1974–present |
| Inaugurated | 1974 |
| Founder | Francis Groux, Jean Mardikian, Claude Moliterni |
| Attendance | around 200,000 on average[[1]](#cite_note-1) and more than 220,000 in 2012[[2]](#cite_note-attendancefigaro-2) |
| Leader | Franck Bondoux[[3]](#cite_note-3) |
| Organized by | 9eART+ Société Organisatrice du Festival |
| Website | [BDAngouleme.com](http://www.bdangouleme.com) |

The **Angoulême International Comics Festival** (**AICF**; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Festival international de la bande dessinée d'Angoulême*, *FIBD*), held in [Angoulême](/wiki/Angoul%C3%AAme "Angoulême"), France, is the second largest [comics](/wiki/Comics "Comics") festival in Europe after the [Lucca Comics & Games](/wiki/Lucca_Comics_%26_Games "Lucca Comics & Games") of Italy, and the third biggest in the world after Lucca and the [Comiket](/wiki/Comiket "Comiket") of Japan.[[4]](#cite_note-official1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-mausangoul-5)[[6]](#cite_note-blcoshtabw-6) It has occurred every year since 1974, on the last weekend of January; the 2026 edition was cancelled amidst an artists' boycott to the festival organizer 9emeArt+, accused of mismanagement.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angoul%C3%AAme_International_Comics_Festival&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Angoulême International Comics Festival was founded by French writers and editors [Francis Groux](/w/index.php?title=Francis_Groux&action=edit&redlink=1 "Francis Groux (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francis_Groux "fr:Francis Groux")] and Jean Mardikian, and comics writer and scholar [Claude Moliterni](/w/index.php?title=Claude_Moliterni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Claude Moliterni (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Moliterni "fr:Claude Moliterni")].[[8]](#cite_note-ActuaBD-8) Moliterni served as co-organizer of the festival through 2005.[[8]](#cite_note-ActuaBD-8)

## Attendance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angoul%C3%AAme_International_Comics_Festival&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Attendance")]

Over 200,000 visitors[[5]](#cite_note-mausangoul-5) attend the fair every year, including between 6,000 and 7,000[[9]](#cite_note-9) professionals including approximately 2500 authors and 800 journalists.[[4]](#cite_note-official1-4)

The attendance is generally difficult to estimate because the festival takes place all over town, and is divided in many different areas that are not connected to each other directly.[[10]](#cite_note-scarcebcasoitc-10)

## Official prizes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angoul%C3%AAme_International_Comics_Festival&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Official prizes")]

The four-day festival is notable for awarding several prestigious prizes in cartooning. The awards at Angoulême were originally called the **Alfred** awards, after the pet [auk](/wiki/Auk "Auk") from *[Zig et Puce](/wiki/Zig_et_Puce "Zig et Puce")* by [Alain Saint-Ogan](/wiki/Alain_Saint-Ogan "Alain Saint-Ogan"). In 1989, the name changed to the **Alph-art** awards, honoring the final, [unfinished *Tintin* album](/wiki/Tintin_and_Alph-Art "Tintin and Alph-Art") by [Hergé](/wiki/Herg%C3%A9 "Hergé"). In 2003, the Alph-art name was dropped, and they are now simply called "The Official Awards of the International Comics Festival" (*le Palmarès Officiel du Festival international de la bande dessinée*). In 2007, [Lewis Trondheim](/wiki/Lewis_Trondheim "Lewis Trondheim") (2006 Grand Prix winner) created a mascot for the festival, *Le Fauve* (The Wildcat), and since 2008 the prize winners have received wildcat statuettes, with the Best Album statuette [coated in gold](/wiki/Gilding "Gilding"). Since this year, the award is called the *fauve* and the best album, the *fauve d'or*. The prizes were reorganized too, to create a pool of 40-60 albums, called "official selections", from which are awarded the "Best Album" prize, five "Angoulême Essentials", one "Revelation Essential" (given to rookie creators), and one Essential chosen by the public. The Heritage Essential (for reprinted material) and Youth Essential are selected from separate nominee pools.

Additionally, the *[Grand Prix de la ville d'Angoulême](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_la_ville_d%27Angoul%C3%AAme "Grand Prix de la ville d'Angoulême")* is awarded each year to a living creator honoring their lifetime achievement, and the Grand Prix winner becomes president of the next year's festival. Traditionally, the president heads the prize jury of the next year's festival, illustrates the festival poster, and is given an exhibition of his or her work. Four women have been awarded the prize: French author [Florence Cestac](/wiki/Florence_Cestac "Florence Cestac"), Japanese mangaka [Rumiko Takahashi](/wiki/Rumiko_Takahashi "Rumiko Takahashi"), Canadian [Julie Doucet](/wiki/Julie_Doucet "Julie Doucet"), and British cartoonist [Posy Simmonds](/wiki/Posy_Simmonds "Posy Simmonds").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Kim_Jung_Gi_Yann_Blake.jpg/250px-Kim_Jung_Gi_Yann_Blake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kim_Jung_Gi_Yann_Blake.jpg)

Artist Kim Jung Gi in [interview](/wiki/Interview "Interview") with Yann Blake in [Angoulême](/wiki/Angoul%C3%AAme "Angoulême") during the festival (January 2019)

## Other prizes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angoul%C3%AAme_International_Comics_Festival&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other prizes")]

* Prize for School Comic
* Prize for Young Talent (Prix Jeunes Talents)
* Prize for Young Talent from the Aquitaine Region
* "Strip" Prize
* Prize of the Students of Poitou-Charentes (secondary school)
* Prize of the Students of Angoulême (primary school)
* Prize for Alternative Comics (fanzines)
* Hippocampus Prize (for creators with disabilities)
* Other prizes have been created on the margins of the festival, known as the Off Of Off.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) These awards are the Prix Tournesol, the [Shlingo](/wiki/Charlie_Schlingo "Charlie Schlingo") Award and the [Prix Couilles-au-cul](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prix_Couilles_au_cul "fr:Prix Couilles au cul"), literally translated as the "Balls to the buttocks" award, deriving from the French slang expression for bravery, and who celebrates and encourages artistic freedom in artists whose activism is repressed in their home countries.
